text: The surgeon uses blunt dissection to remove early flimsy adhesions, which are visible as thin connective tissue bands, formed 48 hours post-dilatation.
The surgeon opens a small window in the tissue, revealing the gallbladder for identification and further dissection.
Dense adhesions are visible, likely resulting from previous cholangitis and cholecystitis, surrounding the gallbladder area.
The surgeon uses sharp dissection to gradually identify the correct anatomical plane, employing a monopolar device with low wattage and short bursts of energy for precise tissue separation.
The surgeon carefully dissects close to the gallbladder, releasing adhesions while avoiding nearby structures such as the colon and duodenum.
The surgeon uses traction and counter traction to fully expose the gallbladder, meticulously releasing adhesions surrounding it.
The surgeon visualizes the entire gallbladder, noting the presence of adhesions between the lateral border of the gallbladder, the liver bed, and the gastrocolic omentum.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the thick-walled and edematous gallbladder, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues using sharp dissection techniques.

The surgeon expands the dissection area to expose the common bile duct, identifying and enlarging the correct tissue plane. Fibrous strands are divided sharply, while remaining adhesions are moved away using blunt dissection techniques.
The surgeon encounters thick adhesions around the gallbladder, which are being divided sharply using either cold scissors or low voltage monopolar energy.
The surgeon applies good traction to the tissue, carefully identifying the vascular plane before using energy devices. Energy is applied in short, conventional bursts to avoid continuous exposure.
The surgeon further dissects the flimsy adhesions located at the lower border of the liver, carefully separating these tissue connections.

The surgeon has exposed the entire gallbladder and hepatodermal ligament area, with the duodenum retracted downward. A small incision is made on the fat covering the hepatodermal ligament, revealing a vein and gradually exposing important structures. Care is taken to maintain the correct surgical plane.
The surgeon hooks the tissues and pulls them away from the structures intended for preservation, before applying energy to dissect the area.
A gauze is placed to keep the surgical field dry, while suction is used for blunt dissection. The artery and vein are clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues dissection by extending the incision cranially between the gallbladder and the omental adhesions, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon carefully incises the peritoneum, ensuring not to penetrate too deeply to avoid the common hepatic duct and hepatic artery. A suction device is used to confirm the correct surgical plane.

The surgeon provides traction on the gallbladder's lateral aspect, identifying the correct dissection plane. Both blunt and sharp dissection techniques are employed to separate the gallbladder from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon meticulously dissects near the posterior surface of the gallbladder, maintaining close proximity to avoid medial damage to the portal structures.
The gallbladder is gradually dissected off the liver bed, revealing the posterior surface. The surgeon then creates a conventional anterior window for further dissection.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum and hooks it, using energy to create a small window. An instrument is passed through this window, which is gradually expanded to facilitate further dissection of the gallbladder from the liver bed.

The surgeon encounters difficulty in dissecting the Calot's triangle and employs a safe technique to ensure thorough dissection around the suspected cystic duct or common bile duct, focusing on both anterior and posterior aspects.
The surgeon continues to dissect the gallbladder from the liver bed, working both anteriorly and posteriorly to open up the window, aiming to achieve the critical view of safety.
The surgeon continues to dissect the gallbladder from the liver bed, even in cases with a frozen callus, ensuring careful separation of the gallbladder tissue from the liver surface.

The surgeon uses an instrument to point out the cystic duct and the common bile duct (CBD) within the created window, demonstrating that no other structures are present between the liver bed and the cystic duct.
The surgeon uses an instrument to gently milk the cystic duct, ensuring no remnant stones are present. The cystic duct appears wide, which may have allowed a stone to pass into the common bile duct.

The surgeon uses a proline suture for intracorporeal ligation at the junction of the gallbladder and cystic duct. Following ligation, scissors are used to incise the cystic duct just below the ligation site.
Obstructed bile flows freely from the cystic duct, which is suctioned out. The surgeon inserts a small infant feeding tube and applies pressure with saline, causing fluid to gush out visibly.

The surgeon further inserts the tube to check for patency, using contrast media to visualize the intrahepatic ducts, common bile duct (CBD), and duodenum, ensuring the dye spills into these structures.

The surgeon performs a polydocotomy, ligating the distal stump of the cystic duct with an endo loop. The gallbladder is carefully dissected from the liver bed, with attention to identifying the correct tissue planes to avoid penetrating the liver parenchyma.

The surgeon inserts a dermia basket through the incision on the cystic duct to retrieve small stones from the common bile duct (CBD), demonstrating a transcystic approach for cholangiogram and stone removal.

The surgeon completes the removal of the gallbladder, noting that the procedure avoids opening the common bile duct (CBD), which minimizes morbidity and results in outcomes similar to a simple laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon uses an endo bag with sutures in a pursing fashion to secure the specimen. The bag is opened up to accommodate the specimen.

The surgeon holds the gallbladder at the cystic duct region and places it into the endobag, positioning the cystic duct portion towards the bag's opening. An incision is made at this level to facilitate the removal of bile or stones, followed by ligation of the duct.
